seo.title: Healthcare RAG Pipeline AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for rag pipeline AI agents in healthcare. Covers HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, HITECH Act.

content.intro: Deploying a RAG pipeline in healthcare environments introduces compounding risks: retrieval errors can surface outdated clinical guidance, LLM hallucinations can fabricate drug interactions or dosage information, and any PHI exposure during processing triggers HIPAA and HITECH liability. This checklist operationalizes the specific controls required to deploy a compliant, auditable, and clinically safe RAG system. Given failure consequences that include patient harm and fines up to $1.5M per violation, every item here should be treated as non-negotiable before go-live.

sections.heading: PHI Handling & Data Governance
sections.description: Ensure all patient health information is properly controlled throughout the RAG pipeline — from document ingestion through vector storage to LLM prompt assembly — in compliance with HIPAA, HITECH, and FDA 21 CFR Part 11.

items.label: PHI De-identification Before LLM Submission
items.description: Implement an automated de-identification layer (using NLP-based PHI detection tools such as AWS Comprehend Medical or Microsoft Presidio) that strips or masks all 18 HIPAA identifiers from retrieved document chunks before they are assembled into LLM prompts. Log every de-identification action with a timestamp and document ID.

items.label: Data Residency Enforcement for Vector Store
items.description: Configure your vector database (e.g., Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) to store embeddings and document chunks exclusively within your approved processing environment — on-premises or a HIPAA-eligible cloud region. Disable all cross-region replication that would move data outside the defined boundary.

items.label: Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) for All Vendors
items.description: Obtain signed BAAs from every third-party vendor in the RAG pipeline that may process PHI, including embedding model providers, vector database vendors, LLM API providers, and monitoring platforms. Maintain a vendor register with BAA expiry dates and annual review cycles.

items.label: Encryption at Rest and In Transit for Document Corpus
items.description: Enforce AES-256 encryption for all stored clinical documents, embeddings, and metadata in the vector store. Require TLS 1.2 or higher for all data in transit between retrieval, context assembly, and LLM inference components. Document encryption key management procedures in your security policy.

items.label: Role-Based Access Control on Document Corpus
items.description: Implement RBAC so that only authorized clinical and engineering roles can ingest, update, or delete documents from the knowledge base. Enforce minimum necessary access principles — a nurse querying drug interactions should not retrieve oncology trial protocols unless clinically indicated.

items.label: Data Retention and Deletion Policy for Retrieved Chunks
items.description: Define and enforce a retention policy for any temporary storage of retrieved document chunks or assembled prompts. Ensure that prompt logs containing de-identified content are purged on a schedule consistent with your HIPAA retention policy (minimum 6 years for covered entities).

items.label: Audit Logging of All Data Access Events
items.description: Log every document retrieval event with user ID, timestamp, query hash, document IDs retrieved, and de-identification status. Store logs in a tamper-evident, write-once system (e.g., AWS CloudTrail with S3 Object Lock) to satisfy FDA 21 CFR Part 11 audit trail requirements.

sections.heading: RAG Pipeline Accuracy & Hallucination Controls
sections.description: Clinical RAG outputs must be grounded in retrieved source documents. Implement technical controls to detect hallucinations, verify source faithfulness, and ensure retrieved context is clinically relevant before synthesis reaches any end user.

items.label: Source Faithfulness Scoring on Every Response
items.description: Integrate a faithfulness evaluation step (e.g., using RAGAS, TruLens, or a custom NLI-based scorer) that measures whether each claim in the LLM output is directly supported by the retrieved document chunks. Set a minimum faithfulness threshold (e.g., 0.85) and block or flag responses that fall below it.

items.label: Hallucination Detection with Medical Knowledge Grounding
items.description: Deploy a secondary validation layer that cross-checks LLM-generated clinical statements (drug names, dosages, ICD codes, contraindications) against a structured medical knowledge base (e.g., RxNorm, SNOMED CT, or a curated formulary). Flag any generated claim that cannot be verified against a structured source.

items.label: Context Relevance Filtering Before LLM Synthesis
items.description: Score each retrieved document chunk for semantic relevance to the query using a reranker model (e.g., Cohere Rerank or a fine-tuned cross-encoder). Discard chunks with relevance scores below a defined threshold before assembling the context window, preventing off-topic or outdated clinical content from influencing the response.

items.label: Citation Accuracy Verification with Span-Level Attribution
items.description: Require the LLM to produce span-level citations linking each factual claim to a specific sentence or paragraph in a retrieved document. Implement a post-processing step that verifies each citation reference exists in the retrieved corpus and that the cited text actually supports the claim made.

items.label: Document Freshness Validation for Clinical Guidelines
items.description: Tag all ingested clinical documents with version numbers, publication dates, and expiry dates. During retrieval, filter out documents that have been superseded or are older than a configurable clinical recency threshold (e.g., clinical guidelines older than 2 years require a freshness review flag).

items.label: Confidence Score Thresholding with Human Escalation
items.description: Define a composite confidence score combining faithfulness, relevance, and citation accuracy. When the composite score falls below a critical threshold, automatically route the query to a licensed clinician for review rather than surfacing the AI-generated response directly to the end user.

items.label: Adversarial Query Testing for Clinical Edge Cases
items.description: Before deployment, run a red-team evaluation with at least 200 adversarial clinical queries (e.g., rare drug interactions, off-label uses, ambiguous symptom presentations) to identify failure modes in retrieval and synthesis. Document results and remediation steps in your validation report.

sections.heading: Auditability & Regulatory Traceability
sections.description: Every AI-assisted clinical decision must be fully reconstructable for regulatory review, malpractice defense, and internal quality assurance. This section covers the logging, versioning, and traceability controls required by HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, and SOC 2 Type II.

items.label: Immutable End-to-End Audit Trail per Query
items.description: For every RAG query, log the full pipeline trace: original query, retrieved document IDs and relevance scores, de-identification actions applied, assembled prompt hash, LLM model version and temperature settings, raw response, post-processing scores, and final output delivered to the user. Store in a tamper-evident log store.

items.label: LLM Model Version Pinning and Change Control
items.description: Pin the LLM model version used in production and implement a formal change control process (aligned with FDA 21 CFR Part 11 Section 11.10) before upgrading. Maintain a model registry documenting model name, version, provider, validation date, and approved clinical use cases for each deployed version.

items.label: Knowledge Base Version Control with Diff Logging
items.description: Treat the clinical document corpus as a versioned artifact. Log every document addition, update, or deletion with the editor's identity, timestamp, and reason for change. Maintain the ability to reconstruct the exact knowledge base state at any historical point in time for retrospective audit.

items.label: User Action Logging for Clinical Decision Support Outputs
items.description: Log whether each AI-generated recommendation was accepted, modified, or overridden by the clinician, along with the clinician's user ID and timestamp. This creates the human-in-the-loop accountability record required for malpractice defense and regulatory inspection.

items.label: SOC 2 Type II Controls Mapping for RAG Components
items.description: Map each RAG pipeline component (vector store, embedding service, LLM API, orchestration layer) to your SOC 2 Type II trust service criteria. Ensure your security controls documentation explicitly covers the availability, confidentiality, and processing integrity of the AI pipeline.

items.label: Periodic Audit Log Review and Anomaly Alerting
items.description: Schedule quarterly reviews of RAG pipeline audit logs by your compliance team. Implement automated anomaly detection (e.g., unusual query volumes, repeated low-confidence responses, access outside business hours) with alerts routed to your security operations center within 15 minutes of detection.

items.label: Incident Response Playbook for AI-Generated Clinical Errors
items.description: Document a specific incident response playbook for scenarios where a RAG pipeline error contributes to a clinical adverse event. Include steps for immediate output quarantine, root cause analysis across the retrieval and synthesis layers, regulatory notification timelines, and patient notification procedures.

sections.heading: Clinical Safety, Validation & Deployment Governance
sections.description: Healthcare AI systems require clinical validation, ongoing performance monitoring, and governance structures that go beyond standard software deployment. These controls ensure the RAG pipeline remains safe and effective across diverse patient populations and clinical contexts.

items.label: Pre-Deployment Clinical Validation Study
items.description: Conduct a prospective validation study with licensed clinicians evaluating RAG outputs against a gold-standard clinical reference set of at least 500 cases. Measure accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and clinician acceptance rate. Document results in a formal validation report before any production use.

items.label: Bias Audit Across Patient Demographics
items.description: Evaluate RAG pipeline outputs for performance disparities across patient age groups, sex, race/ethnicity, and socioeconomic indicators. Use a structured bias evaluation framework (e.g., disaggregated performance metrics per demographic subgroup) and remediate any statistically significant disparities before deployment.

items.label: Clinician Training and Competency Assessment
items.description: Require all clinical users of the RAG system to complete a structured training program covering system capabilities, known limitations, how to interpret confidence scores and citations, and when to escalate to human review. Assess competency with a post-training evaluation before granting system access.

items.label: Continuous Post-Deployment Performance Monitoring
items.description: Implement a real-time monitoring dashboard tracking faithfulness scores, hallucination detection rates, citation accuracy, query latency, and clinician override rates on a rolling 7-day and 30-day basis. Set alert thresholds that trigger a clinical safety review if any metric degrades by more than 10% from baseline.

items.label: Scope Limitation and Out-of-Scope Query Blocking
items.description: Define the approved clinical scope of the RAG system (e.g., drug interaction lookup, clinical guideline retrieval) and implement a query classification layer that detects and blocks out-of-scope queries (e.g., diagnostic conclusions, treatment prescriptions) with an appropriate user-facing message and escalation path.

items.label: Third-Party Penetration Testing of RAG Infrastructure
items.description: Commission an annual third-party penetration test specifically targeting the RAG pipeline components: prompt injection attacks, vector store data exfiltration, embedding inversion attacks, and unauthorized document corpus access. Remediate all critical and high findings within 30 days of the test report.

items.label: Clinical Governance Committee Oversight
items.description: Establish a clinical governance committee with representation from clinical informatics, legal, compliance, and practicing clinicians that reviews RAG system performance quarterly, approves knowledge base updates, and has authority to suspend the system if patient safety concerns are identified.

items.label: FDA Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) Classification Review
items.description: Engage regulatory counsel to assess whether your RAG pipeline meets the FDA's definition of Software as a Medical Device under 21 CFR. If the system influences clinical diagnosis or treatment decisions, determine whether a 510(k) submission or De Novo request is required before deployment.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: HIPAA Privacy Rule (45 CFR §164.514) requires that all 18 PHI identifiers be removed or generalized before data is considered de-identified. For RAG pipelines, this means your de-identification layer must be validated against the Safe Harbor or Expert Determination method, and the validation must be documented. Using an LLM to de-identify PHI before sending it to another LLM does not satisfy this requirement unless the de-identification model itself is validated and operates within your HIPAA-compliant environment.

FDA 21 CFR Part 11 mandates that electronic records used in clinical decision-making have audit trails that are computer-generated, time-stamped, and tamper-evident. For RAG pipelines, this means your query logs, retrieved document records, and LLM output logs must meet Part 11 standards if the system is used in any FDA-regulated clinical context (e.g., clinical trials, drug administration decisions). Ensure your log storage system is validated under 21 CFR Part 11 Section 11.10(e).

The HITECH Act (42 U.S.C. § 17931) strengthens HIPAA enforcement and extends liability to business associates. If your RAG pipeline uses any third-party embedding, retrieval, or LLM service that processes PHI — even transiently — that vendor is a business associate and must have a signed BAA. HITECH also requires breach notification within 60 days of discovery; ensure your incident response playbook includes a specific trigger for RAG pipeline data exposure events.

SOC 2 Type II certification for your RAG infrastructure requires demonstrating that controls for security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy have been operating effectively over a minimum 6-month observation period. For AI systems, auditors increasingly scrutinize model change management, training data provenance, and output monitoring as part of processing integrity criteria. Ensure your model version control, knowledge base change logs, and performance monitoring dashboards are explicitly included in your SOC 2 scope documentation.

cta.heading: Automate Your Healthcare Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your RAG pipeline against HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, and HITECH controls — catching PHI exposure risks, hallucination spikes, and audit gaps before they become violations.
